subject: Security Center Advisory
body: PayPal PayPal is committed to maintaining a safe environment for its community of customers. To protect the security of your account, PayPal employs some of the most advanced security systems in the world and our anti-fraud teams regularly screen the PayPal system for unusual activity. We are contacting you to remind you that our Account Review Team identified some unusual activity in your account. In accordance with PayPal's User Agreement and to ensure that your account has not been compromised, access to your account was limited. Your account access will remain limited until this issue has been resolved. To secure your account and quickly restore full access, we may require some additional information from you for the following reason: We have been notified that a card associated with your account has been reported as lost or stolen, or that there were additional problems with your card. This process is mandatory, and if not completed within the nearest time your account or credit card may be subject for temporary suspension. To securely confirm your PayPal information please click on the link bellow: We encourage you to log in and perform the steps necessary to restore your account access as soon as possible. Allowing your account access to remain limited for an extended period of time may result in further limitations on the use of your account and possible account closure. For more information about how to protect your account please visit PayPal Security Center. We apologize for any incovenience this may cause, and we apriciate your assistance in helping us to maintain the integrity of the entire PayPal system. Thank you for using PayPal! The PayPal Team

body:
Dear Isabella,

Thank you for your email and insightful suggestion! We appreciate you taking the time to share your feedback with us.

We understand how integrating IAM roles with Cloud Management permissions could bring significant value to your workflow. It's definitely an interesting concept and aligns well with our goal of providing a seamless and secure platform.

We've forwarded your suggestion to our product development team for further evaluation. While we can't promise immediate implementation, we'll certainly keep your feedback in mind as we plan future enhancements.

In the meantime, please let us know if you have any other questions or suggestions.

Best regards,

Aetheros Support Team

subject: Re: Urgent: IAM role permissions not applying correctly
body:
Hi John,

Thank you for contacting Aetheros Support.

We understand that you are experiencing issues with IAM role permissions. To help us investigate further, could you please provide us with the following information:

* The specific role name and permissions that are not being applied correctly.
* The resources that users are able to access when they should not have access.
* Any recent changes made to the role or permissions.

Once we have this information, we will be able to assist you further.

Best regards,
Aetheros Support Team

subject: Will renters rise up in Toronto’s election?
body: First Up Plus: Who gets the final say on a listing price? ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ LIVING HERE By Amber Shortt Good morning. Here’s the latest on cottage country’s new normal, how renters could shape the Toronto election and who should get final say on a listing price. GOOD WEEK FOR... Homebuilders. A jump in sales shows buyers are trickling back to the GTA’s newly built home market. But, as BILD’s CEO says, now is not the time to call the market “anywhere near being balanced” — we still need more housing. Homebuyers. Bidding wars are back — but if you have the means and a strong stomach, the Star’s David Olive says it’s a good time to get into the market. Here's why this time is different. Dockside deskers. Elon Musk may still have a few fans, as hybrid workers in Ontario’s cottage country hail his satellite internet service as a “game changer.” It’s one factor in cottage country’s new normal. Where should you eat next? If you’re wondering where to eat as the city heats up, you need Food Crawl, the Star’s free food newsletter. Whether it’s a strip mall standout or a downtown gem, Food Crawl will make you an insider on the best eats in Toronto and beyond. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... Caledon residents, who are caught in the middle of the Peel Region divorce. It’s meant to help speed up housing, but sorting out the details will likely be a headache. Indebted Canadians. Canadians have racked up more household debt than any other G7 country, with about three-quarters stemming from mortgages — and it will likely get worse. Young investors. Not surprisingly, a new Statistics Canada report shows the real estate investment market is dominated by boomers, with the number of homes investor-owned at 15 per cent. Here’s who else is investing in Canadian real estate. TRENDING During the last municipal election, a candidate knocked on my door and told my partner not to sell our house, listing all the reasons property values in our former city of York neighbourhood would be rising. And when my partner told him we were renting? “Oh,” he said. “That’s too bad.” I’d say I can’t blame him for being surprised. To most people, the idea of a renter conjures up an image of an undergrad student in a basement eating Kraft Dinner — not a family of four in a detached house, well, also eating Kraft Dinner (parents of three-year-olds, you get it). But not only is the number of renting households increasing, it’s also becoming more diverse — meaning renters could be a force in next month’s mayoral election. “It’s everyone — young professionals, families, seniors — and they’re all struggling because Toronto is not an affordable city to live in,” tenant advocate Douglas Kwan told the Star’s Victoria Gibson and May Warren this week. Tenants’ frustration is mounting over rising rents, threats of eviction and, for some, the lost dream of home ownership as prices rise again. Mayoral hopefuls this time around are waking up to the notion that the 48 per cent of Toronto households who pay rent instead of a mortgage could decide their fate. But as policies to increase housing affordability roll off candidates’ tongues, the big question may not be whether renters vote, but will they vote together? As University of Toronto professor Matti Siemiatycki told the Star, it’s not a foregone conclusion that renters will vote as a block, saying, “Whether it’s urban and suburban, or motorist and cyclist and pedestrian, there are all sorts of other dynamics that people see as important to their identity and interests.” Still, as the housing crisis deepens, any candidate who ignores renters’ concerns may just find tenants have no interest in giving them a four-year lease on the mayor’s seat. And that’s too bad. LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND “Drive until you qualify” is pushing home hunters farther from the city and to places like Bradford, Ont., where this home doubled in price in a decade. The Residences of the Well at Front St. and Spadina Ave. can only be rented, not bought by investors. One renter, life coach Katia Stern, shares why it works for her. This Richmond Street East building now boasts Canada’s first residential air-to-water heat pump — technology that could make all condos carbon-free. Check it out. Remember that Kensington laneway home with a rooftop shower? It’s still for sale for a slightly lower asking price of just under $3 million. Soak it in. [Realtor.ca] Whitby’s heritage committee decided a home built in the late 1800s, with no discernible historical value, can be torn down for a new housing complex. [DurhamRegion.com] In Vienna’s Gemeindebauten (social housing), the proportion of tenants’ incomes that goes to rent is in the single digits. What can Toronto learn? [The New York Times] SHOULD I OVERRULE MY AGENT ON LIST PRICE? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS The Star talked to Property.ca broker Julian Kashani about how agents decide on a list price, and what happens when an agent and seller disagree. Here’s what he said: When suggesting a price to a client, I usually do a comparative market analysis to get a sense of what similar properties have recently sold for. I even take my client to other currently listed properties in their area and show them the pluses and minuses of a property compared to their own. If we do disagree on price, I advise we price it a little below what we think the property is worth but do an offer date — only accepting offers on one day — and see what the market will offer. This sales strategy is often done in hot sellers’ markets and when it is hard to price a property because it is unique. If the client wants to list at a higher price, thinking we can lower it later if needed, I explain they risk staying in the market too long, which could lead buyers to wonder if there’s something wrong with the property. An agent’s job is to give their client the best possible information, but at the end of the day it’s their property and the price is their choice. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Before you go, here’s a look inside the moated community of Toronto Islands. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week. Or, if you'd prefer, you can unsubscribe from this newsletter by clicking the first link below. subject: [zzzzteana] Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls
body:
Ananova:
Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls

A man has admitted making hoax calls to police investigating the
disappearance of Soham schoolgirls Jessica Chapman and Holly Wells.
Wrexham Magistrates Court, in North Wales, heard jobless Howard Youde made
three calls to police in Cambridgeshire claiming to have abducted the
youngsters.
He was arrested in Wrexham in the early hours of August 16 when officers
traced the call to a phone box on the town's Brook Street.
The 45-year-old, of Queensway, Hope, near Wrexham, has pleaded guilty to one
count of wasting police time on August 15 this year.
The court was told Youde claimed to have no recollection of making the calls
having been drinking all day.
Defence lawyer Mark Arden says the offence was neither premeditated nor
calculated but added that this was no excuse.
He said: "What he's done is horrific. It's unforgivable. The distress he's
caused the families is unacceptable."
Youde has been released on unconditional bail until November 7 when he will
be sentenced.
The hearing has been adjourned for pre-sentence reports although the
defendant has been warned custody is an option.
Story filed: 12:36 Monday 7th October 2002
